---
title: My dynamic report
output: html_fragment
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)

library(tidyverse)

# Create data frame from the published CSV file
logged_data <- read_csv(url("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/e/2PACX-1vSdCw6VAWohCjomqMRjgfmZs8T7Uq1Dfg6rj761s33p6x8UNFaTP-WXVEvn9983u4h3ckpSdEBn0Ayu/pub?output=csv"))

# Create a new data frame with renamed variables/columns
latest_data <- logged_data %>%
  select(-1, -2) %>%
  rename(employment_status = 1,
         age_group = 2,
         freq_days = 3,
         estimated_min = 4,
         activity_type = 5,
         urgency_rating = 6)

# Modify employment_status to list non-standard responses as "Other"
latest_data <- latest_data %>%
  mutate(employment_status = str_remove(employment_status, "\\."),
         employment_status = case_when(
           str_squish(employment_status) %in% c("Full-time employed", 
                                                "Part-time employed", 
                                                "Student", 
                                                "Unemployed", 
                                                "Retired") ~ employment_status,
           is.na(employment_status) ~ NA,  # Making sure NA values are coerced as "Other"
           TRUE ~ "Other"))

# Calculate total time on OLA (Offline Leisure Activity) per respondent my element-wise vector multiplication
# BUT FIRST: convert categorical freq_days response bands into numerical midpoint equivalents 
latest_data <- latest_data %>%
  mutate(
    freq_numeric = case_when(
      freq_days == "0 days." ~ 0,
      freq_days == "1-2 days." ~ 1.5,
      freq_days == "3-4 days." ~ 3.5,
      freq_days == "5-6 days." ~ 5.5,
      freq_days == "7 days." ~ 7
    ) 
  )

latest_data <- latest_data %>%
  mutate(
    weekly_min = estimated_min * freq_numeric
  )

# VARIABLES OF INTEREST (VOI) & auto remove NAs just in case
# 1 - find average median time spent on OLA (to avoid outlier effects)
median_time_spent <- latest_data$weekly_min %>%
  median(na.rm = TRUE)

# 2 - find average median frequency of engagement in OLA (to avoid outlier effects)
median_frequency <- latest_data$freq_numeric %>%
  median(na.rm = TRUE)

# 3 - find most common response-band for urgency_rating
mode_urgency <- which.max(table(latest_data$urgency_rating))

# 4 - find most popular activity type
# Remove space at the start and end of each row in activity_type column for ease
mode_activity <- latest_data %>%
  separate_rows(activity_type, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(activity_type = str_trim(activity_type)) %>%
  count(activity_type, sort = TRUE)


# NUMBER OF RESPONSES/POPULATION
population <- nrow(latest_data)


# MOST POPULAR OLAs RANKED converted to single vector without n
ranked_act <- c(mode_activity$activity_type)


# PLOT SECTION

# Plot employment status vs. avg_weekly_min
# Summarise data first for AVG weekly_min by employment_status
employment_summary <- latest_data %>%
  group_by(employment_status) %>%
  summarise(avg_weekly_min = median(weekly_min, na.rm = TRUE)) # Used median calculation because of huge right-skewing outliers

weekly_by_status <- employment_summary %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = employment_status,
             y = avg_weekly_min)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") + # Lets my bar graph use my specified y = avg_weekly_min
  labs(title = "Median Weekly OLA Time by Employment Status",
       x = "Employment Status",
       y = "Median Weekly OLA Time (minutes)",
       caption = "Source: Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")

# Plot raw weekly OLA time by urgency ratings to observe correlative relationship
weekly_by_urgency <- latest_data %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_point(aes(x = urgency_rating,
                 y = weekly_min)) +
  labs(title = "Raw Data on Weekly OLA Time by Urgency Ratings",
       x = "Urgency Yating (1-5)",
       y = "Weekly OLA Time (minutes)",
       caption = "Source: Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")

# Plot count (i.e., no. of respondents that selected for an urgency rating), by employment status
# Summarise data first to create one bar per urgency rating
urgency_count_summary <- latest_data %>%
  group_by(urgency_rating, employment_status) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = 'drop')

urgency_by_employment <- urgency_count_summary %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_bar(aes(x = urgency_rating,
               fill = employment_status)) +
  labs(title = "Reported Urgency Ratings by Respondents According to Employment Status",
       x = "Urgency rating (1 - 5 scale in ascending order of urgency)",
       y = "Count of Responses",
       caption = "Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")

```

```{css}

@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Bungee&display=swap');

@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Cormorant+Garamond:ital,wght@1,600&family=Momo+Trust+Display&family=Momo+Trust+Sans:wght@200..800&display=swap');

h1{
 font-family: "Bungee", sans-serif;
 font-size: 5rem;
 color: #FFD700;
 text-shadow: 2px 2px 0 black;
}

h2 {
  font-family: "Bungee", sans-serif;
  font-size: 3rem;
  color: #FFD700;
  text-shadow: 2px 2px 0 black;
    }
    
h3 {
  font-family: "Bungee", sans-serif;
  color: #FFD700;
  text-shadow: 2px 2px 0 black;
  margin-top: 1em;
  margin-bottom: 0.2em;
    }

@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Cormorant+Garamond:ital,wght@1,600&family=Momo+Trust+Display&display=swap');

p, li {
  font-family: "Momo Trust Sans", sans-serif;
  font-weight: 300;  /* Light weight */
}

table,
figure,
.r-plot img{
  border: 2px solid #000000 !important;
  border-radius: 12px;
  display: block;
  margin-left: auto;
  margin-right: auto;
  width: 80%; !important;
  height: auto; !important;
}

img {
  border: 3px solid #000000 !important;
  border-radius: 12px;
  display: block;
  margin-left: auto;
  margin-right: auto;
}

.markdown-img{
  border: 3px solid #000000 !important;
  border-radius: 12px;
  display: block;
  margin-left: auto;
  margin-right: auto;
  max-width: 33%; !important;
  }

.section-border {
  border: 3px solid #000000 !important;
  border-radius: 12px;
  padding: 25px;
  margin: 20px 0;
  background: #f9f9f9;
}

```

::: {.section-border}
 
```{r}
# AVERAGE TIME SPENT ON OLAs (median to avoid outlier effects)
med_min <- median.default(latest_data$estimated_min)

# MOST POPULAR OLAs RANKED converted to single vector without n
ranked_act <- c(mode_activity$activity_type)
```

## Dynamic Report On Offline Leisure Activity (OLA) Habits of Aucklanders

 
Link to my GitHub Pages published dynamic report:
[Gabriel's GitHub Pages/stats220 public repository](https://github.com/jgrn716/stats220)
 
### Interesting Insights: 
***How Much Time Does the Average Kiwi Spend on OLAs?***

Amongst the `r population` respondents, the average (*median*) time spent per week on OLA was `r median_time_spent` minutes; or `r median_time_spent/60` hours. This means that the average Kiwi, according to my small sample, typically spends `r med_min` minutes; or `r med_min/60` hours on a given day that they engage in offline leisure activities. 

![An animated image of a popular character, "Crayon Shin-chan", dancing.](https://tenor.com/en-NZ/view/shinchan-dance-shinchan-짱구-짱구춤-dance-gif-25219440.gif) 
 \newline
 \newline

***Top Five OLA Hobbies:***

According to my survey, the *top five* OLA hobbies amongst Kiwis are: 
1. `r c(ranked_act[1])` 
2. `r c(ranked_act[2])` 
3. `r c(ranked_act[3])` 
4. `r c(ranked_act[4])` 
5. `r c(ranked_act[5])` 
 \newline
 \newline

### Plotted Visualisations
 
```{r weekly-by-status-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=14, out.width="80%", fig.cap="Figure 1: A bar graph depicting the median amount of time (minutes) respondents spent on OLAs.", echo=FALSE}

# Summarise data first for AVG weekly_min by employment_status
employment_summary <- latest_data %>%
  group_by(employment_status) %>%
  summarise(avg_weekly_min = median(weekly_min, na.rm = TRUE)) # Used median calculation because of huge right-skewing outliers

# Plot employment status vs. avg_weekly_min
ggplot(employment_summary, aes(x = employment_status,
                               y = avg_weekly_min)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") + # Lets my bar graph use my specified y = avg_weekly_min
  labs(title = "Median Weekly OLA Time by Employment Status",
       x = "Employment Status",
       y = "Median Weekly OLA Time (minutes)",
       caption = "Source: Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")
```


Results: Respondents who selected for the "Game developer, casual warehouse assistant", and "Part-time employed" statuses engaged in OLAs significantly more than their peers (i.e., "Full-time employed", "Unemployed", and "Freelancer"). It is important to consider that, due to the small sample size (*n* = 20), there is a very assymmetric distribution of employment statuses skewingly strongly towards "Full-time employed" responses. Additionally, significant time constraints for this project resulted in a very short interval between data collection and analysis. This provides the added risk of bias in the types of individuals who responded to the survey.
 \newline
 \newline
 \newline



```{r weekly-by-urgency-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=14, out.width="80%", fig.cap="Figure 2: A scatterplot depicting the distribution of raw OLA time allocation values (minutes per week), with respect to urgency rating response-bands.", echo=FALSE}

# Plot raw weekly OLA time by urgency ratings to observe correlative relationship
ggplot(latest_data) +
  geom_point(aes(x = urgency_rating,
                 y = weekly_min)) +
  labs(title = "Raw Data on Weekly OLA Time by Urgency Ratings",
       x = "Urgency Rating (1-5)",
       y = "Weekly OLA Time (minutes)",
       caption = "Source: Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")
```


Results: Urgency rating responses appear to cluster around the values of "3" and "4", out of the 5-point scale. This indicates that most Kiwis (from this population), feel moderate to moderately-high levels of urgency when engaging in OLAs. If these effects do extend to wider populations, it is of interest whether the current geopolitical, and socioeconomic climate contributed to these results, versus modern cultural shifts in lifestyle pressures, versus a combination of both - or an entirely other factor. Additionally, not accounting for outliers, there appears to be a generally inversely proportional correlation between weekly OLA time and urgency rating. That is, the higher the respondents rated their feelings of urgency - whilst engaging in OLAs - the less time they engaged in OLAs throughout the week. Once again, it is important to consider that due to the small sample size and asymmetric distribution of employment status responses, this correlative relationship seems to disappear. 
 \newline  
 \newline  
 \newline  
 
 
 
```{r urgency-by-employment-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=14, out.width="80%", fig.cap="Figure 3: A bar graph depicting the number of responses with respect to urgency rating response-bands, for each employment status category.", echo=FALSE}

# Summarise data first to create one bar per urgency rating
urgency_count_summary <- latest_data %>%
  group_by(urgency_rating, employment_status) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = 'drop')

# Plot count (i.e., no. of respondents that selected for an urgency rating), by employment status
ggplot(urgency_count_summary) +
  geom_bar(aes(x = urgency_rating,
               y = count,
               fill = employment_status),
           stat = "identity") +
  labs(title = "Reported Urgency Ratings by Respondents According to Employment Status",
       x = "Urgency rating (1 - 5 scale in ascending order of urgency)",
       y = "Count of Responses",
       caption = "Offline Leisure Activity Habits of New Zealanders: A Survey")
```


Results: Interestingly, respondents who classified themselves as either "Full-time employed" or "Part-time employed" are the most well-distributed across urgency rating response-bands. On the other hand, the urgency ratings of the respondents classified as "Freelancer" and "Game developer, casual warehouse assistant" are congruent with the prior result indicating an average tendency to score between "3" and "4" for urgency ratings. Meanwhile, the respondent classified as "Unemployed" selected for the lowest urgency rating (i.e., "1").  These results of the latter three cohorts however, have limited power and generalisability as all three belonged to a sample size of *n* = 1 each. Being that the "Full-time employed" and "Part-time employed" cohorts were the largest contributes to the sample size of this study,it would be inappropriate to discuss their effect on this pattern. 
***
 \newline  
 \newline  
 \newline  
:::
